# Architecture Conformance Standard

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Standard

Every system SHOULD declare how its code is organized into the components
and boundaries of its stated architecture, and that declared structure
SHOULD match reality. The mapping from code unit to architectural
component is explicit, discoverable, and checkable.

Architecture that exists only in documents while the code is organized
some other way is not architecture — it is aspiration. When the map and
the territory disagree, every reader is misled: the new contributor who
trusts the docs, the agent that reasons from the declared structure, and
the reviewer who assumes a change is contained when it is not. The
remedy is not more documentation; it is a declared, checked mapping
between the architecture a system claims and the way its code is
actually arranged.

## What Good Looks Like

- The system's architecture is declared in a machine-readable form
  (which components/boundaries exist, and what each is responsible for).
- Every code unit maps to exactly one declared component. No code lives
  in an undeclared catch-all bucket.
- The declared dependency direction between components is explicit, and
  the actual code respects it (this is where Acyclic Dependencies, JOS-P20,
  is enforced concretely).
- The mapping is checkable: a tool can compare declared structure against
  actual structure and report drift, rather than relying on inspection.
- New code has an obvious, declared home before it is written — the
  structure answers "where does this go?" so the answer is not "wherever
  is easiest," which is how catch-all buckets form.
- When the architecture changes, the mapping is updated deliberately as
  part of the change, not discovered to be stale months later.

## Gap Analysis Guide

- **No declared architecture.** Structure lives only in folder names and
  tribal knowledge. There is nothing to conform to. Critical gap.
- **Declared but unmapped.** An architecture document exists, but no
  explicit mapping ties code units to its components. Conformance cannot
  be checked.
- **Mapped but unchecked.** A mapping exists but nothing verifies it, so
  it silently drifts as code moves and grows.
- **Catch-all accumulation.** A single undeclared bucket (a generic
  "services", "utils", "misc", or "common" grouping) steadily absorbs
  code that has no declared home. The clearest symptom of a missing or
  unenforced conformance discipline.
- **Dependency direction undeclared.** Components depend on each other in
  whatever way is locally convenient; cycles form invisibly.

## 1970/2070 Note

A 1970 engineering team building a system from a block diagram expected
the built system to match the diagram — a subsystem on the drawing
corresponded to an identifiable assembly in the machine, and you could
point to where each part lived. A 2070 team composing AI-generated
modules at high velocity needs the same guarantee even more, because no
human reads every file: the declared structure is the only reliable map
of a system too large to hold in one head. The principle is constant —
the way a system is built must match the way it says it is built, and
that correspondence must be checkable, not assumed.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain standard extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer standards. It is an
adoption-instrumentation addition to the engineering domain established
by JOS-D0047: it specializes the existing principle "Prefer Explicit
Boundaries" (JOS-P14) into a checkable expectation for code structure.
It deliberately governs only the era-neutral requirement (declare your
structure, keep it matched, make it checkable) and does NOT prescribe a
language, a folder layout, or a checking tool — those are venture-level
instance decisions per the template/instance split (JOS-FW-001 §7).

## Connected Objects

- JOS-P14 — Prefer Explicit Boundaries (the principle this standard implements)
- JOS-P20 — Acyclic Dependencies (declared dependency direction)
- JOS-C03 — Boundary Management (foundational concept)
- JOS-C25 — Abstraction Leakage (undeclared structure is leaked architecture)
- JOS-D0049 — Universal Base Manifest (the code/component declaration this checks)

### Framework Connection

Framework §8 (the boundary contract, specifically the Interface slot —
what a component exposes and what it depends on) provides the structural
machinery: conformance is the check that a code unit's realized interface
and placement match the slot it claims to fill. §7 (template vs instance)
locates this standard's authority — the conformance requirement is the
JOS-level template; each venture's declared structure and checking tool
are the instance.
